title: Muhammad
summary: The Prophet of Islam — born in Mecca around 570 CE, a merchant who received revelations he identified as the word of God, who united the Arabian Peninsula under a new monotheism, and whose life, teaching, and example became the foundation of a civilization that at its height stretched from Spain to Central Asia and remains a living force for 1.8 billion people — and a figure whose historical reconstruction is entangled with theological commitment in ways that make him one of the most studied and most contested persons in history.
hook: Muhammad is the most influential person in history who left no written record. Everything we know about him comes from sources compiled generations after his death, by communities with strong reasons to present him in specific ways. The Quran — which Muslims hold to be God's word, not Muhammad's — was assembled into canonical form under the third Caliph. The hadiths — reports of his sayings and deeds — were compiled in the 9th century, two hundred years after his death, by scholars who were simultaneously doing history and theology. The historical Muhammad and the theological Muhammad cannot be fully separated, and the attempt to do so is itself a contested act.

beginner.theFigure:
Muhammad ibn Abdullah was born in Mecca, in the Hejaz region of the Arabian Peninsula, around 570 CE — the traditional date, though the sources are imprecise. He was orphaned early, raised by an uncle, and became a merchant. He married Khadijah, a wealthy widow who employed him, and the marriage appears to have been genuinely loving; he took no other wives during her lifetime. He was known in Mecca as al-Amin — the trustworthy one.

At around age forty, in the cave of Hira outside Mecca, he began receiving what he understood to be divine revelations — communicated through the angel Gabriel. The first word revealed was 'Read' (or 'Recite'). He was initially terrified; Khadijah reassured him and became the first Muslim. For the next twenty-three years, until his death, he continued receiving revelations that were memorized by his followers and eventually compiled into the Quran.
beginner.worldInherited:
Muhammad inherited a world in which Arabia was peripheral to the great powers of Byzantium and Sasanian Persia. The Arabian Peninsula was tribal, largely polytheist, with significant Jewish and Christian communities. Mecca was a commercial and religious center — the Kaaba, a cube-shaped shrine, housed multiple deities and attracted pilgrims from across Arabia. The Quraysh tribe, to which Muhammad belonged, controlled Mecca and the Kaaba's revenues.

The context of late antique monotheism — the spread of Christianity, Judaism, and Zoroastrianism across the Near East — shaped the environment in which Islam emerged. Muhammad's initial message was recognizably part of this monotheist tradition: one God, moral accountability, final judgment, the importance of prayer and almsgiving. His specific claim — that the revelations he received were God's final and definitive message to humanity, superseding earlier revelations — distinguished Islam from the traditions it drew on.
beginner.howExercisedPower:
The initial response to Muhammad's preaching in Mecca was hostility. He challenged the city's polytheism — and therefore the economic basis of the Quraysh's control of the Kaaba. His followers were persecuted; some fled to Christian Abyssinia. In 622 CE, Muhammad and his followers migrated to Medina — the hijra that marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar. In Medina he became a political leader as well as a prophet. The Constitution of Medina established a community (umma) encompassing the Muslim emigrants, the Medinan converts (ansar), and the Jewish tribes of Medina.

The following decade saw continuous political and military activity. The Muslims fought the Meccans at Badr (624, a Muslim victory), Uhud (625, a Muslim defeat), and the Battle of the Trench (627, a successful defense of Medina). The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah (628) produced a strategic pause. In 630, Muhammad led a force of ten thousand to Mecca — the city surrendered without significant fighting. He destroyed the idols in the Kaaba and declared it the house of God. Two years later, he died in Medina.
beginner.whatTheyChanged:
Muhammad's death in 632 left the question of succession unresolved — the source of the Sunni-Shia split that has divided Islam ever since. The first four caliphs — Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali — led the explosive expansion of the early Islamic community. Within a century of Muhammad's death, Muslim armies had conquered Arabia, the Levant, Egypt, Persia, Central Asia, and much of Spain. This was one of the most rapid political and cultural transformations in human history.

Islamic civilization was built on the foundation Muhammad established. The Quran is the word of God. The Prophet's sunnah — his practice — is the model for human conduct. The Five Pillars (testimony of faith, prayer, almsgiving, fasting, pilgrimage) form the basic structure of religious life. That foundation supported an extraordinary flowering of theology, philosophy, mathematics, medicine, and art.
beginner.legendVsRecord:
The historical reconstruction of Muhammad's life faces a fundamental evidential problem. The earliest biography (Ibn Ishaq's Sirat Rasul Allah) was compiled about 130 years after his death from earlier materials, many of which have not survived. The hadith collections were compiled 200 years after Muhammad's death. The classical hadith scholars themselves recognized that many were spurious. They developed sophisticated methods to evaluate chains of transmission.

Patricia Crone and Michael Cook's Hagarism (1977) argued, provocatively, that Islamic origins differed substantially from the traditional account. The early Islamic movement, they claimed, was originally oriented toward Jerusalem rather than Mecca. Much of the traditional narrative, they suggested, was constructed retrospectively. This argument has been substantially contested but it sharpened scholarly attention to the evidentiary problems. Montgomery Watt's two-volume biography (Muhammad at Mecca, 1953; Muhammad at Medina, 1956) remains the most balanced scholarly synthesis.

general.theFigure: The scholarly study of Muhammad involves navigating between two equally problematic approaches: accepting the traditional Islamic account uncritically, or dismissing it systematically in search of a 'real' historical figure beneath the tradition. Fred Donner's Muhammad and the Believers (2010) takes a middle approach — treating the early Islamic movement as a 'believers' movement' that initially included Christians and Jews who accepted Muhammad's prophetic authority, and that only gradually defined itself as a distinct religion in the generation after his death. This reading has been influential in recasting questions about Islam's relationship to earlier monotheism.
general.worldInherited: The late antique context for Islamic origins has been substantially developed by scholars including Robert Hoyland (Arabia and the Arabs, 2001) and Chase Robinson. The traditional Islamic account treats Arabian paganism as the primary context for Muhammad's mission. Recent scholarship has emphasized the extent to which Arabia was integrated into late antique monotheist culture — through Jewish and Christian communities, through Zoroastrian influence, and through Arabia's position on trade routes connecting the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean worlds. Understanding Muhammad requires understanding this context as much as the specifically Arabian one.
general.howExercisedPower: The Constitution of Medina — a document that most scholars accept as authentic, though debating its precise dating and scope — is the most significant political text from Muhammad's lifetime. It established the umma as a political community with defined rights and obligations for different groups (Muslim emigrants, Medinan converts, Jewish tribes), specified dispute resolution procedures, and created the framework for collective defense. The document reveals a Muhammad who was a sophisticated political actor operating within the conventions of Arabian tribal politics while also transcending them.
general.whatTheyChanged: The question of what drove the early Islamic conquests — whether they were primarily religiously motivated, tribally driven, or economically prompted — has been one of the most debated in early Islamic history. The traditional account emphasizes religious motivation: spreading God's message. Fred Donner's The Early Islamic Conquests (1981) argues for a combination of religious ideology and the social dynamics of tribal Arabia. Robert Hoyland's In God's Path (2015) emphasizes the role of tribal confederations that had already been militarizing in the late antique period. The conquests remain one of the most dramatic and consequential military-political transformations in history, and their explanation remains contested.
general.legendVsRecord: Karen Armstrong's Muhammad: A Biography of the Prophet (1992) represents the sympathetic scholarly-popular account — situating Muhammad in his historical context, taking the tradition seriously while acknowledging its limitations, and arguing for a Muhammad who was not primarily a military figure but a spiritual and social reformer responding to the specific conditions of 7th-century Arabia. The contrast with Salman Rushdie's fictional treatment in The Satanic Verses (1988) — which triggered a fatwa and years of death threats — illustrates the continuing political sensitivity of representations of Muhammad in ways that representations of most historical figures do not.

advanced.theFigure: The question of whether a historical Muhammad is recoverable from the sources has been one of the most technically demanding in the study of Islamic origins. The 'skeptical' approach — associated with John Wansbrough, Patricia Crone, and Michael Cook — argued that the Islamic tradition was largely a later construction and that the historical Muhammad is largely irrecoverable. This approach has been substantially criticized on methodological grounds: the argument from silence (the absence of contemporary documentary evidence does not mean the events did not occur) and the internal evidence of the early sources (which show signs of genuine historical memory).
advanced.worldInherited: The archaeology of 7th-century Arabia — substantially advanced in recent decades through surveys and excavations in Saudi Arabia, Yemen, and Jordan — has provided a material context for understanding the world in which Islam emerged. The evidence for trade networks, settlement patterns, and religious practices partially corroborates and partially complicates the literary sources. The emergence of a distinctive material culture associated with early Islam — in coinage, inscriptions, and architecture — can be traced archaeologically from the late 7th century onward.
advanced.howExercisedPower: The Sunni-Shia split — originating in the dispute over succession after Muhammad's death — has been analyzed extensively as both a theological and a political division. Ali ibn Abi Talib (Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law) was passed over for the caliphate three times before becoming the fourth caliph, then was assassinated. His son Hussein was killed at Karbala in 680. The Shia tradition of mourning for Hussein — Ashura — became one of the most powerful ritual expressions in Islam. Whether the original split was primarily political (a dispute about legitimate succession) or theological (a dispute about the nature of the imamate) remains debated.
advanced.whatTheyChanged: The legal elaboration of the sunnah — the development of fiqh (Islamic jurisprudence) based on the Quran and Muhammad's practice — was one of the most consequential intellectual achievements of the early Islamic period. The four major Sunni legal schools (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) developed different methodologies for deriving legal rulings from the sources. The hadith criticism tradition — developing methods to evaluate the authenticity of reports about Muhammad based on their chains of transmission — was a sophisticated historical-critical practice developed centuries before European source criticism.
advanced.legendVsRecord: The ongoing debate about depicting Muhammad — whether visual representation is permissible — illustrates how Muhammad's image and legacy remain live political and theological questions. The traditional Islamic prohibition on depicting the Prophet (not Quranic but derived from hadith and theological reflection) has generated controversies from the Danish cartoon controversy (2005–06) to the Charlie Hebdo attacks (2015). These events illustrate how a 7th-century figure's representation remains a site of intense conflict in the contemporary world — and how the question of who has the authority to represent Muhammad, and in what form, is inseparable from broader questions about Islam's place in global culture.

foundation.theStory: Muhammad matters as the founder of Islam — one of the world's great religions with 1.8 billion adherents — and as a historical figure whose reconstruction is entangled with theological commitment in ways that make him both the most important and one of the most difficult figures in religious history to understand. Understanding him requires distinguishing what can be established historically from what Muslims affirm theologically, without treating the theological tradition as merely legendary.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the reliability of the sources for reconstructing Muhammad's life, the relationship between Islam and earlier monotheism (was Islam a new religion or a development of existing traditions?), the causes of the early Islamic conquests, and the ongoing question of what Muhammad's example (sunnah) requires of Muslims today.
foundation.whyItMatters: Muhammad matters for understanding Islam, the history of monotheism, the early Islamic conquests, the Sunni-Shia split, the relationship between religion and political authority, and the ongoing debates about Islam's place in the contemporary world.
questions: The hadith — reports of Muhammad's sayings and deeds compiled two centuries after his death — are the primary source for Islamic law and practice beyond what the Quran explicitly requires. Classical hadith scholars developed sophisticated methods to evaluate authenticity. Modern critical scholars argue that many hadiths cannot be reliably attributed to Muhammad. How should Muslims and historians use sources whose authenticity is uncertain but whose authority is foundational?

Muhammad was both a prophet and a statesman — a religious leader who also exercised political and military authority over a community. This combination has generated both a model for Islamic political thought (the idea that religion and political authority are properly unified) and endless debate about what that model requires in different historical contexts. Is the combination of prophetic and political authority a contingent feature of Muhammad's specific situation, or a normative model for Islamic governance?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential scholarly accounts are Montgomery Watt's two-volume biography (the most balanced historical synthesis), Fred Donner's Muhammad and the Believers (the most innovative recent account), and Karen Armstrong's Muhammad (the most accessible sympathetic account). Patricia Crone and Michael Cook's Hagarism is essential for understanding the skeptical tradition. The Quran itself — in a good translation (Abdel Haleem's Oxford World's Classics edition is widely recommended) — is indispensable.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary situation for Muhammad's life is substantially worse than for most major historical figures of comparable importance. The Quran is authentic but tells us less about Muhammad's biography than about his teaching. The biography tradition (sira) and hadith collections are rich but late and theologically motivated. Contemporary non-Muslim sources are sparse and often hostile. The main interpretive challenge is using these sources critically without either uncritical acceptance or systematic skepticism.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Muhammad must engage with the source problem directly — taking a position on how much of the traditional account is historically reliable and what methodologies allow recovery of the historical figure. It must address the Islamic-origins question — whether Islam is best understood as a development of late antique monotheism or as something genuinely new. And it must engage with the political theology question — what the relationship between prophetic and political authority in Muhammad's career implies for Islamic political thought.
questions: The Constitution of Medina — if authentic — reveals a Muhammad who created a pluralist political community encompassing Jews and pagan Arabs as well as Muslims. The subsequent expulsion and extermination of Medina's Jewish tribes (the Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qurayza) seems to contradict this. How should historians reconcile these apparently different approaches to non-Muslim communities in Muhammad's Medina?

Donner argues that the early Islamic movement was initially a 'believers' movement' that included Christians and Jews — that the sharp boundary between Islam and earlier monotheism was established only in the generation after Muhammad. If this is correct, what does it imply for how we understand Islam's relationship to Judaism and Christianity, and for the theological claim that Islam is the final and complete revelation?

research.topic: The early Islamic conquests and their causes
research.content: The early Islamic conquests — the rapid expansion of Muslim political control across Arabia, the Levant, Egypt, Persia, and Central Asia within a century of Muhammad's death — are among the best-documented events of late antiquity, with sources from multiple traditions (Arabic, Greek, Syriac, Persian). The sequence and approximate dates of the conquests are well established. The explanation for their speed and extent remains debated: religious motivation, tribally organized military capacity, the exhaustion of Byzantine and Sasanian Persia by decades of mutual warfare, and the administrative flexibility of the early Islamic polity have all been proposed.

research.topic: The archaeology of early Islam and the material evidence for Islamic origins
research.content: The archaeology of early Islamic Arabia — substantially constrained until recently by Saudi restrictions on excavation at sacred sites — has been opened up by recent surveys and excavations. The evidence for pre-Islamic Arabia's trade networks, settlement patterns, and religious practices provides material context for understanding the world in which Islam emerged. Early Islamic material culture — coinage, inscriptions, architecture — can be traced from the late 7th century onward and partially corroborates the literary tradition while also complicating it in specific ways.

What evidence would resolve this: first-person accounts of Muhammad's experience of receiving revelation — what the revelatory experience felt like, how he understood the relationship between his own consciousness and the divine communication he was receiving, and what his private doubts (if any) were. Why unavailable: Muhammad left no written personal record; the Quran is presented as divine speech, not personal reflection; the hadith tradition records his reported words and deeds but not his inner experience; and the tradition has strong reasons to present his religious experience in specific ways. What can be established: the content of the revelations, the traditional accounts of how he received them, and the responses of those around him. What cannot be established: his subjective experience of revelation or any private uncertainty about its nature.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Seal of the Prophets
comparativeNarrative.content: The traditional Islamic account reads Muhammad as the final prophet in a long line of divine messengers — Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and others — sent by God to guide humanity. His revelation is final and complete, superseding earlier revelations that had been corrupted or misunderstood. His life is the perfect model for human conduct — to be imitated in prayer, in social relations, in governance, in commerce. The hadith tradition preserves this model in detail. On this reading, understanding Muhammad requires engagement with the tradition as a living religious resource, not merely as historical evidence.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Arabian Prophet
comparativeNarrative.content: The Western Orientalist tradition — from medieval Christian polemics through 18th and 19th century scholarship to early 20th century works — read Muhammad as a human figure shaped by the specific conditions of 7th-century Arabia. The positive version (Watt, Armstrong) emphasizes his genuine spiritual insight, his social reformism, and his political genius — arguing that his achievements are comprehensible in historical terms without requiring miraculous explanation. The negative version (common in medieval and early modern Christian writing) read him as a false prophet, an impostor, or a deluded visionary. Said's Orientalism (1978) analyzed how Western scholarship's framing of Muhammad reflected colonial power relations rather than neutral inquiry.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Problem of Sources
comparativeNarrative.content: The critical historical tradition — represented by Crone, Cook, Wansbrough, and their successors — takes seriously the evidentiary problems in reconstructing Muhammad's life and is skeptical of conclusions that exceed what the contemporary evidence supports. On this reading, much of the traditional account reflects the concerns of the 9th-century scholars who compiled it rather than the 7th-century reality it claims to describe. The historical Muhammad is significantly less accessible than the tradition suggests — not necessarily different from the traditional account, but not independently verifiable. This methodological skepticism has been criticized for excessive negativity and for the impossibility of its standard of proof.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Islam is the religion Muhammad founded — its theology, practice, and civilization all derive from the Quran he received and the sunnah he exemplified.

rabbitHole.label: Mecca
rabbitHole.reason: Mecca was Muhammad's birthplace, the site of his initial preaching, the city he fled and eventually conquered, and the location of the Kaaba which he rededicated to the one God.

rabbitHole.label: Monotheism
rabbitHole.reason: Muhammad's message was fundamentally monotheist — the affirmation that there is one God (Allah) and that Muhammad is his Messenger places Islam within the tradition of Abrahamic monotheism while claiming to complete and correct earlier revelations.

rabbitHole.label: The Islamic Golden Age
rabbitHole.reason: The Islamic Golden Age — the extraordinary intellectual and cultural flowering of the 8th through 13th centuries — was built on the foundations Muhammad established, both the theological and legal framework of Islam and the political unity of the early caliphate.

rabbitHole.label: The Crusades
rabbitHole.reason: The Crusades were fought over Jerusalem and the Holy Land — territory central to Muhammad's religious geography — and represent the most violent sustained encounter between the civilization Muhammad founded and the Christian West.

rabbitHole.label: The Hebrew Bible / Old Testament
rabbitHole.reason: The Hebrew Bible's figures — Abraham, Moses, David, Solomon, and others — appear in the Quran as prophets in the same divine tradition that culminates in Muhammad. Islam's relationship to the Hebrew Bible is simultaneously one of continuity and supersession.

rabbitHole.label: Siddhartha Gautama
rabbitHole.reason: Muhammad and the Buddha represent the two great religious founders of the Axial Age and its aftermath — both men whose teachings generated global civilizations, both known primarily through traditions compiled after their deaths, and both whose historical reconstruction is entangled with theological commitment.

reference.author: Watt, W. Montgomery
reference.title: Muhammad at Mecca / Muhammad at Medina
reference.annotation: The most balanced scholarly biography — Watt's two-volume account remains the standard historical synthesis, engaging the sources critically while taking the tradition seriously.

reference.author: Armstrong, Karen
reference.title: Muhammad: A Biography of the Prophet
reference.annotation: The most accessible sympathetic account — Armstrong situates Muhammad in his historical context with scholarly rigor and genuine empathy for the religious tradition.

reference.author: Donner, Fred
reference.title: Muhammad and the Believers: At the Origins of Islam
reference.annotation: The most innovative recent account — Donner's 'believers' movement thesis substantially revises how Islam's relationship to earlier monotheism is understood.

commerce.title: Muhammad: A Biography of the Prophet
commerce.author: Karen Armstrong
commerce.note: Start here — Armstrong situates Muhammad in his historical context with scholarly rigor and genuine empathy for the religious tradition; the most accessible sympathetic account and the right starting point for readers from any background.

commerce.title: Muhammad and the Believers: At the Origins of Islam
commerce.author: Fred Donner
commerce.note: The most important recent scholarly reinterpretation — Donner's 'believers' movement thesis argues that early Islam was initially inclusive of Christians and Jews who accepted Muhammad's authority; substantially revises how Islam's relationship to earlier monotheism is understood.

commerce.title: Muhammad at Mecca / Muhammad at Medina
commerce.author: W. Montgomery Watt
commerce.note: The essential scholarly biography — Watt's two-volume account remains the most balanced historical synthesis; engages the sources critically while taking the tradition seriously; the standard for academic biography of Muhammad.

commerce.title: The Quran
commerce.author: Translated by M.A.S. Abdel Haleem
commerce.note: Read the primary source — the Quran is indispensable for understanding Muhammad; Abdel Haleem's Oxford World's Classics translation is widely recommended for accessibility and accuracy; reading even the shorter suras reveals the texture of the revelation.

commerce.title: In God's Path: The Arab Conquests and the Creation of an Islamic Empire
commerce.author: Robert Hoyland
commerce.note: The essential account of what Muhammad's mission produced — Hoyland's history of the early Islamic conquests uses all available sources (including non-Muslim ones) to reconstruct the most dramatic political transformation of the 7th century.

popularCulture.title: The Five Pillars of Islam
popularCulture.description: The Five Pillars — the Shahada (testimony of faith: 'There is no god but God, and Muhammad is his Messenger'), Salat (prayer five times daily facing Mecca), Zakat (obligatory almsgiving), Sawm (fasting during Ramadan), and Hajj (pilgrimage to Mecca for those who are able) — are the practical framework of Islamic life that Muhammad established. They are observed daily by 1.8 billion people in forms that have remained remarkably stable for fourteen centuries. The Hajj draws up to 2.5 million pilgrims annually — the largest regular human gathering on Earth — to the same sites Muhammad visited. The Five Pillars are the most direct connection between Muhammad's life and the daily lives of his followers across 57 majority-Muslim countries.

popularCulture.title: Aniconism, depiction, and the politics of representation
popularCulture.description: The traditional Islamic prohibition on depicting Muhammad — derived from hadith rather than explicit Quranic instruction, and interpreted with varying strictness across Muslim traditions and centuries — reflects a theological commitment to avoiding idolatry that runs through Islamic art broadly. This aniconism produced the extraordinary tradition of Islamic geometric and calligraphic art as alternatives to figural representation. The prohibition became politically acute in the 21st century when Western publications produced caricatures of the Prophet. The Danish newspaper Jyllands-Posten's cartoons (2005) and the Charlie Hebdo attacks (2015) forced questions about freedom of expression, religious respect, and whether secular and religious communities can share public space on terms both accept. For Muslims, these controversies were not primarily about offense taken but about the theological significance of the prohibition — a distinction the secular framing of the debate often missed. Understanding aniconism requires understanding it as a living theological commitment rather than a sensitivity to insult.

popularCulture.title: The Hajj as lived religion
popularCulture.description: The annual Hajj — the pilgrimage to Mecca required once in a lifetime for every Muslim who is physically and financially able — is the world's largest annual gathering of humanity and the most direct enactment of Muhammad's legacy in contemporary life. Pilgrims perform rituals that Muhammad performed and commanded: circling the Kaaba, running between the hills of Safa and Marwa, standing at the plain of Arafat, stoning the pillars at Mina. The Hajj is simultaneously a religious obligation, a global demonstration of Islamic unity, and a massive logistical challenge that Saudi Arabia manages with varying success. Malcolm X's description of his 1964 Hajj — in which he encountered a racial diversity that transformed his views on race in America — is one of the most famous accounts of what the pilgrimage can mean beyond its ritual requirements.

popularCulture.title: Islam as civilizational achievement
popularCulture.description: The civilization that followed from Muhammad's mission — preserving Greek philosophy, developing algebra and astronomy, creating networks of trade from Spain to Indonesia, producing architecture from the Alhambra to the Taj Mahal — is the most consequential legacy of his life by almost any measure. The Islamic Golden Age (roughly 8th–13th centuries) produced advances in mathematics, medicine, optics, and philosophy that were transmitted to European scholars and contributed to the Renaissance and Scientific Revolution. This civilizational achievement is often presented separately from Muhammad himself, but it was built on the political and religious framework he established: the community of believers, the Arabic language of the Quran as a lingua franca, and the Islamic legal and scholarly tradition that preserved and extended ancient knowledge.

popularCulture.title: Islam and modernity
popularCulture.description: The relationship between Islam — as Muhammad defined its foundational texts and practices — and the political and cultural frameworks of modernity is the most consequential ongoing debate within Muslim communities worldwide. Does shari'a (Islamic law, derived from the Quran and Muhammad's sunnah) require specific political arrangements incompatible with liberal democracy? Can Muhammad's example be applied to 21st-century contexts he could not have anticipated? These questions are debated by Islamic scholars, political movements, and governments from Morocco to Indonesia. The diversity of answers — from Islamist movements that seek to restore an idealized early Islamic political order to liberal Muslim scholars who read Muhammad's example as requiring constant reinterpretation — reflects the genuine openness of the foundational texts to different readings.
